# Is nuclear power cost-competitive with solar and wind?

On levelized cost alone, new nuclear is not competitive with new solar or wind in most markets. That comparison is incomplete, because the three technologies deliver different products, and the gap narrows once system-level costs of variability are priced in — though in most published system studies it does not close.

## Levelized cost

Lazard's 2024 estimates put unsubsidized utility-scale solar at roughly $29-92/MWh and onshore wind at $27-73/MWh, against $142-222/MWh for new nuclear. The nuclear range reflects recent Western construction; Vogtle Units 3 and 4 came in at roughly $35 billion against an initial $14 billion estimate.

Costs outside the West differ materially. South Korean and Chinese builds have completed closer to schedule and budget, and the literature attributes much of the gap to construction experience and design standardization rather than to anything intrinsic to the technology.

## Why levelized cost is an incomplete comparison

Levelized cost divides lifetime cost by lifetime output and is silent on *when* that output arrives. Solar and wind produce on their own schedule; nuclear produces on demand within its ramping limits. At low penetration this distinction is minor, because the existing system absorbs the variability. At high penetration it is not, and the marginal value of additional variable generation falls as its output correlates with output already on the system.

## System-level studies

Studies that model whole systems rather than individual plants generally find a role for firm low-carbon capacity, and generally find that role is smaller than nuclear advocates project and larger than levelized-cost comparisons imply. Sepulveda et al. (2018) find that firm low-carbon resources reduce total system cost across most of their scenario space, with the size of the effect depending heavily on assumed storage costs.

The honest summary is that the answer depends on penetration level, storage cost trajectory, and local construction capability, and that no single number answers it.

## Sources

- Lazard (2024). *Levelized Cost of Energy+*, version 17.0.
- Sepulveda, N. A., Jenkins, J. D., de Sisternes, F. J., & Lester, R. K. (2018). The Role of Firm Low-Carbon Electricity Resources in Deep Decarbonization of Power Generation. *Joule*, 2(11), 2403-2420.
